commit 5beb4caabe5bf57ac137baf568ac77568d6fadd8
Author: Kit Dallege <xaum.io@gmail.com>

Docs/mm: document Page Reclaim

This commit fills in the page_reclaim.rst stub as part of the structured
memory management documentation.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst b/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst
> index 50a30b7f8ac3..bfa53bee98c2 100644
> --- a/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst

[ ... ]

> +Working Set Detection
> +=====================
> +
> +When a page is evicted, a compact shadow entry is stored in its place in
> +the page cache or swap cache.  The shadow records the eviction timestamp
> +(in terms of the lruvec's nonresident age counter) and the cgroup and
> +node that owned the page.
> +
> +If the page is faulted back in (a "refault"), the shadow entry allows the
> +kernel to compute the *refault distance* — how many other pages were
> +activated or evicted between this page's eviction and its refault.  If the
> +refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list, the page
> +was part of the active working set and is immediately activated rather
> +than placed on the inactive list.  This reduces thrashing by protecting
> +frequently accessed pages that would otherwise be repeatedly evicted and
> +refaulted.

Is this description of the refault distance comparison accurate?

Looking at workingset_test_recent() in mm/workingset.c, the refault distance
is actually compared against the workingset_size (the active working set),
rather than the inactive list.

For example, for file pages, the threshold is calculated from NR_ACTIVE_FILE
and explicitly excludes the inactive list of the same type. 

Could the documentation be updated to reflect that the distance is compared
against the size of the active list instead of the inactive list?

diff --git a/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst b/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst
index 50a30b7f8ac3..bfa53bee98c2 100644
--- a/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst
+++ b/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst
@@ -3,3 +3,167 @@
 ============
 Page Reclaim
 ============
+
+Page reclaim frees memory by evicting pages that can be reloaded from disk
+or regenerated.  File-backed pages are dropped (clean) or written back
+(dirty); anonymous pages are swapped out.  The bulk of the implementation
+is in ``mm/vmscan.c``.
+
+.. contents:: :local:
+
+When Reclaim Runs
+=================
+
+Reclaim is triggered in two ways:
+
+- **kswapd**: a per-node kernel thread that runs in the background when
+  free pages in any zone drop below the low watermark.  It reclaims until
+  free pages reach the high watermark, then sleeps.
+
+- **Direct reclaim**: when an allocation cannot be satisfied even after
+  kswapd has been woken, the allocating task reclaims pages synchronously
+  in its own context.  This adds latency to the allocation but is necessary
+  when background reclaim cannot keep up.
+
+Reclaim Priority
+================
+
+The reclaim path operates at decreasing priority levels (from
+``DEF_PRIORITY`` down to 0).  At each level, a larger fraction of the LRU
+lists is scanned.  At the default priority, only 1/4096th of pages are
+considered; at priority 0, the entire list is scanned.
+
+If a full scan at priority 0 still does not free enough memory, the OOM
+killer is invoked (see Documentation/mm/oom.rst).  This escalation
+prevents the system from spinning indefinitely in reclaim.
+
+Scan Control
+============
+
+Each reclaim invocation is parameterized by a ``struct scan_control`` that
+captures the allocation context: which GFP flags were used, how many pages
+are needed, which node or memory cgroup to reclaim from, and whether
+writeback or swap are allowed.  This struct threads through the entire
+reclaim stack, ensuring consistent policy at every level.
+
+LRU Lists
+=========
+
+Each ``lruvec`` (one per node, or per node and memory cgroup combination)
+maintains lists of pages ordered by access recency.
+
+Classic LRU
+-----------
+
+The classic scheme uses four LRU lists per lruvec: active and inactive for
+both anonymous and file-backed pages.  This approximates a second-chance
+(clock) algorithm:
+
+- Pages start on the inactive list when first allocated.
+- If accessed again while on the inactive list, they are promoted to the
+  active list.
+- Reclaim scans the inactive list and evicts pages that have not been
+  recently accessed.
+- To prevent the active list from growing without bound, pages are
+  periodically demoted from active to inactive.
+
+The split between anonymous and file-backed lists allows the reclaim path
+to balance eviction pressure between the two types based on their relative
+cost.  Swapping anonymous pages is generally more expensive than dropping
+clean file pages, so the scanner adjusts the ratio using IO cost
+accounting and the ``vm.swappiness`` tunable.
+
+Multi-Gen LRU
+-------------
+
+The multi-gen LRU is an alternative reclaim algorithm that groups pages
+into generations by access time rather than a simple active/inactive
+split.  It is documented separately in Documentation/mm/multigen_lru.rst.
+
+LRU Batching
+------------
+
+To avoid taking the lruvec lock on every page access, LRU operations are
+batched per-CPU (``mm/swap.c``).  Functions like ``folio_add_lru()`` and
+``folio_mark_accessed()`` queue pages into per-CPU folio batches that are
+drained to the actual LRU lists periodically or when the batch is full.
+This batching is critical for scalability on systems with many CPUs.
+
+Reclaiming Pages
+================
+
+The core reclaim loop (``shrink_node()``) divides its work between page
+cache / anonymous pages and slab caches.  For each lruvec, it scans the
+inactive LRU lists, evaluating each page:
+
+- **Clean file pages** can be dropped immediately — they can be re-read
+  from disk.
+- **Dirty file pages** are queued for writeback.  Reclaim typically skips
+  them and returns later, but under severe pressure it may wait for
+  writeback to complete.
+- **Anonymous pages** are swapped out if swap space is available and
+  ``vm.swappiness`` allows it.
+- **Mapped pages** require TLB invalidation (unmapping) before they can
+  be freed.  The rmap (reverse mapping) system is used to find and
+  remove all page table entries pointing to the page.
+- **Unevictable pages** (locked with ``mlock()``) are skipped entirely.
+  See Documentation/mm/unevictable-lru.rst.
+
+Memory Cgroup Reclaim
+---------------------
+
+When memory cgroup limits are exceeded, reclaim targets only the pages
+belonging to that cgroup.  Each memory cgroup has its own lruvec per node,
+so the scanner can isolate its pages without disturbing the rest of the
+system.  ``try_to_free_mem_cgroup_pages()`` is the entry point for
+cgroup-scoped reclaim.
+
+NUMA Demotion
+-------------
+
+On systems with tiered memory (e.g., fast DRAM and slower persistent
+memory), reclaim can demote pages to a slower tier instead of evicting
+them.  This keeps the data in memory but frees the faster tier for
+actively accessed pages.
+
+Shrinkers
+=========
+
+Besides page cache and anonymous pages, kernel caches (dentries, inodes,
+and driver-specific caches) are reclaimed through the shrinker interface
+(``mm/shrinker.c``).  A shrinker registers two callbacks:
+
+- ``count_objects()``: report how many objects are reclaimable.
+- ``scan_objects()``: free up to a requested number of objects.
+
+The reclaim path calls all registered shrinkers proportionally to the
+amount of reclaimable memory they report.  Shrinkers are NUMA-aware: on
+NUMA systems, each shrinker is called with the node being reclaimed so it
+can prioritize freeing objects local to that node.
+
+Per-memcg shrinker tracking uses bitmap arrays (``shrinker_info``) so that
+the reclaim path only invokes shrinkers that actually have objects in the
+target cgroup, avoiding unnecessary work when there are many cgroups.
+
+Working Set Detection
+=====================
+
+When a page is evicted, a compact shadow entry is stored in its place in
+the page cache or swap cache.  The shadow records the eviction timestamp
+(in terms of the lruvec's nonresident age counter) and the cgroup and
+node that owned the page.
+
+If the page is faulted back in (a "refault"), the shadow entry allows the
+kernel to compute the *refault distance* — how many other pages were
+activated or evicted between this page's eviction and its refault.  If the
+refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list, the page
+was part of the active working set and is immediately activated rather
+than placed on the inactive list.  This reduces thrashing by protecting
+frequently accessed pages that would otherwise be repeatedly evicted and
+refaulted.
+
+Shadow entries consume a small amount of memory.  To prevent them from
+accumulating indefinitely, a shrinker reclaims shadow entries from page
+cache radix tree nodes that contain only shadows and no actual pages.
+
+This logic is implemented in ``mm/workingset.c``.

Is there any issue with this section?
```rst
+Shrinkers
+=========
+
+Besides page cache and anonymous pages, kernel caches (dentries, inodes,
+and driver-specific caches) are reclaimed through the shrinker interface
+(``mm/shrinker.c``).  A shrinker registers two callbacks:
+
+- ``count_objects()``: report how many objects are reclaimable.
+- ``scan_objects()``: free up to a requested number of objects.
+
+The reclaim path calls all registered shrinkers proportionally to the
+amount of reclaimable memory they report.  Shrinkers are NUMA-aware: on
+NUMA systems, each shrinker is called with the node being reclaimed so it
+can prioritize freeing objects local to that node.
+
+Per-memcg shrinker tracking uses bitmap arrays (``shrinker_info``) so that
+the reclaim path only invokes shrinkers that actually have objects in the
+target cgroup, avoiding unnecessary work when there are many cgroups.
```

```rst
+Working Set Detection
+=====================
+
+When a page is evicted, a compact shadow entry is stored in its place in
+the page cache or swap cache.  The shadow records the eviction timestamp
+(in terms of the lruvec's nonresident age counter) and the cgroup and
+node that owned the page.
```
This is true!

```rst
+If the page is faulted back in (a "refault"), the shadow entry allows the
+kernel to compute the *refault distance* — how many other pages were
+activated or evicted between this page's eviction and its refault.  If the
+refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list, the page
+was part of the active working set and is immediately activated rather
+than placed on the inactive list.  This reduces thrashing by protecting
+frequently accessed pages that would otherwise be repeatedly evicted and
+refaulted.
```
Actually, the check is `refault distance <= size of the active list`! Let's check `workingset_refault` logic in `mm/workingset.c`.

/**

...

			workingset_size += lruvec_page_state(eviction_lruvec,
						     NR_INACTIVE_ANON);

	mem_cgroup_put(eviction_memcg);

```

But `workingset_size` uses `NR_ACTIVE_FILE` (and `NR_ACTIVE_ANON`), NOT `NR_INACTIVE_FILE`!

    "content": "FS_A:Yes! `workingset_size` for file pages begins as `lruvec_page_state(eviction_lruvec, NR_ACTIVE_FILE)`. Then it may add anon if swap is available.
So the "active list", not the "inactive list".
The patch text says: "If the refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list, the page was part of the active working set".
This is factually incorrect. The refault distance represents how many evictions happened between when the page was evicted and when it was refaulted. To protect the active working set, the distance is compared against the *active list* size (or the total workingset size), NOT the inactive list size!
Think about it: the inactive list is the staging area. The active list is the working set. If the distance is smaller than the active list, it means if the active list was slightly larger, the page would have been on it. It implies the page is part of a working set that is currently slightly too large to fit in memory, or it was recently active. Actually, the distance is compared against `NR_ACTIVE_FILE` (and optionally anon), which represents the active working set size.
So:
"If the refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list" is WRONG.
It should be: "shorter than the size of the active list" or "shorter than the size of the active working set".
Let's verify this mathematically based on `mm/workingset.c`.
For a file page, `workingset_size = NR_ACTIVE_FILE`. (Plus `NR_ACTIVE_ANON` and `NR_INACTIVE_ANON` if swap is enabled).
For an anon page, `workingset_size = NR_ACTIVE_FILE + NR_INACTIVE_FILE + NR_ACTIVE_ANON`.
In all cases, it's compared to the active list / total working set size, definitely not just the "inactive list" size.
Why would it be the inactive list? If a page is active, it gets demoted to inactive, then evicted. So the number of evictions since its eviction is compared to the active list, meaning if it had stayed on the active list, would it still be there. Yes.

Let's check the text exactly:
`If the refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list, the page was part of the active working set and is immediately activated rather than placed on the inactive list.`

Yes, "size of the inactive list" is incorrect. It should be "size of the active list". This is a significant documentation bug because it misrepresents a core mm algorithm (workingset refault detection algorithm). The algorithm compares refault distance to the active list (plus anon sometimes), so that frequently accessed pages that are thrashing can be protected.

Is there any other inaccuracy?
Let's check "Working Set Detection" paragraph:
`The shadow records the eviction timestamp (in terms of the lruvec's nonresident age counter) and the cgroup and node that owned the page.`
It uses `nonresident age counter` now? Let's check `workingset.c`.
In `workingset_eviction()`:
```c
	eviction = atomic_long_read(&lruvec->nonresident_age);
```
Yes, it does use `nonresident_age`! This was changed in a commit "mm: workingset: age nonresident information based on eviction".
So that part is accurate.

Let's check the "Reclaim Priority" paragraph:
`The reclaim path operates at decreasing priority levels (from DEF_PRIORITY down to 0). At each level, a larger fraction of the LRU lists is scanned. At the default priority, only 1/4096th of pages are considered; at priority 0, the entire list is scanned.`

DEF_PRIORITY is 12.
$2^{12} = 4096$. So 1/4096th is correct.

Let's check the "LRU Batching" paragraph:
`Functions like folio_add_lru() and folio_mark_accessed() queue pages into per-CPU folio batches`
Wait, does `folio_mark_accessed()` queue pages into batches?

    "content": "FS_A:Yes, DEF_PRIORITY is 12.

So the main issue is with the workingset section.
"If the refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list, the page was part of the active working set and is immediately activated rather than placed on the inactive list."
The comparison in `workingset_refault` is against the workingset size (active lists). It does not use the size of the inactive list.
Wait, let me double check the exact algorithm of working set tracking.
From `mm/workingset.c` block comment:
```
 * The workingset size is the size of the active list.
```
If distance <= active list size, it's activated.
Why does the author of the commit say "shorter than the size of the inactive list"?
Maybe in an older version of the kernel?
No, Mel Gorman's book says something similar? Wait, the algorithm has always been "refault distance <= active list".
Wait, `refault_distance <= workingset_size` and `workingset_size = lruvec_page_state(eviction_lruvec, NR_ACTIVE_FILE);`
If it's shorter than the size of the *active list*, we activate it.

Wait, is there anything else?
Let's check `Scan Control` section:
`which node or memory cgroup to reclaim from`
`struct scan_control` has `target_mem_cgroup`, `nodemask`, etc.

Let's check `NUMA Demotion`:
`On systems with tiered memory (e.g., fast DRAM and slower persistent memory), reclaim can demote pages to a slower tier instead of evicting them. This keeps the data in memory but frees the faster tier for actively accessed pages.`
This is implemented in `mm/vmscan.c` via `demote_folio_list()`.

Let's check `Shrinkers`:
`- count_objects(): report how many objects are reclaimable.
- scan_objects(): free up to a requested number of objects.`
Yes, shrinkers have `count_objects` and `scan_objects`.

Is there anything else?
Look at "Reclaiming Pages" section:
`- Clean file pages can be dropped immediately — they can be re-read from disk.`
`- Dirty file pages are queued for writeback. Reclaim typically skips them and returns later, but under severe pressure it may wait for writeback to complete.`
Wait, does reclaim queue them for writeback? In `shrink_folio_list`, if `folio_is_file_lru`, and dirty, we might call `folio_start_writeback` or just queue them in `pageout`. Wait, usually `pageout()` is called for dirty pages, which calls `mapping->a_ops->writepage`.
But actually, the documentation says "queued for writeback". The code does call `pageout()`.

What about Anonymous pages?
`- Anonymous pages are swapped out if swap space is available and vm.swappiness allows it.`
If it allows it. `shrink_folio_list` checks `folio_test_anon` and then does `add_to_swap`.

What about Mapped pages?
`- Mapped pages require TLB invalidation (unmapping) before they can be freed. The rmap (reverse mapping) system is used to find and remove all page table entries pointing to the page.`
This happens in `try_to_unmap()`.

What about Unevictable pages?
`- Unevictable pages (locked with mlock()) are skipped entirely. See Documentation/mm/unevictable-lru.rst.`
Yes.

Let's re-read the workingset text:
`If the page is faulted back in (a "refault"), the shadow entry allows the kernel to compute the *refault distance* — how many other pages were activated or evicted between this page's eviction and its refault. If the refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list, the page was part of the active working set and is immediately activated rather than placed on the inactive list.`

Wait, `refault distance` is measured in terms of `nonresident age` counter, which counts evictions (and activations?). Actually `nonresident_age` counts evictions.
Let's check `workingset_eviction`.
```c
	eviction = atomic_long_read(&lruvec->nonresident_age);
	eviction >>= bucket_order;
	workingset_age_nonresident(lruvec, folio_nr_pages(folio));
```
And `workingset_age_nonresident`:
```c
void workingset_age_nonresident(struct lruvec *lruvec, unsigned long nr_pages)
{
	atomic_long_add(nr_pages, &lruvec->nonresident_age);
}
```
It counts the number of pages evicted!
But the text says:
"how many other pages were activated or evicted between this page's eviction and its refault."
Wait, does it count activations?
No, `nonresident_age` only counts evictions.
Ah, `workingset_activation` used to increment `nonresident_age`? Let's check `workingset_activation`.

    "content": "FS_A:Okay, so `workingset_activation` *does* increment `nonresident_age`! (by calling `workingset_age_nonresident(folio_lruvec(folio), folio_nr_pages(folio));`).
And `workingset_activation` is called in `folio_mark_accessed` when a page is activated.
So the text: "how many other pages were activated or evicted between this page's eviction and its refault" is completely accurate! Both evictions and activations increase `nonresident_age`.

Let's re-examine: "If the refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list, the page was part of the active working set and is immediately activated rather than placed on the inactive list."
The bug here is "shorter than the size of the inactive list". It's the active list.
If you think about it, `nonresident_age` increases on eviction (when page leaves inactive list) and on activation (when page enters active list from inactive list). This pushes pages down the LRU.
The "refault distance" is compared against the *active list* size (`workingset_size`), because we want to see if the page would have survived if the active list was slightly larger. Wait, actually, the distance represents how much active+inactive traffic happened. If distance is smaller than active list size, it means it could fit in the active list (if it hadn't been pushed out by inactive list entries being activated, etc).
So it is definitely the "active list".

    "content": "FS_A:Yes, it exists.

What about: "For each lruvec, it scans the inactive LRU lists, evaluating each page:"
Does it scan the active lists as well?
Yes, `shrink_folio_list` is what scans the lists. But wait, `shrink_node()` first shrinks active lists by demoting pages to inactive list, then it scans the inactive LRU lists. So "For each lruvec, it scans the inactive LRU lists" is basically true (as the actual eviction comes from scanning the inactive lists).

Is there any claim about MGLRU? "The multi-gen LRU is an alternative reclaim algorithm... It is documented separately in Documentation/mm/multigen_lru.rst."
Wait, `multigen_lru.rst` was actually found inside `Documentation/mm/`. Let's re-verify the name. I found it in `Documentation/mm/` directory list. Yes, `multigen_lru.rst`.

Let's check "This batching is critical for scalability on systems with many CPUs."
Yes.

Let's check "NUMA Demotion"
"On systems with tiered memory (e.g., fast DRAM and slower persistent memory), reclaim can demote pages to a slower tier instead of evicting them. This keeps the data in memory but frees the faster tier for actively accessed pages."
Yes.

Let's check "Shrinkers"
"The reclaim path calls all registered shrinkers proportionally to the amount of reclaimable memory they report. Shrinkers are NUMA-aware: on NUMA systems, each shrinker is called with the node being reclaimed so it can prioritize freeing objects local to that node.
Per-memcg shrinker tracking uses bitmap arrays (shrinker_info) so that the reclaim path only invokes shrinkers that actually have objects in the target cgroup, avoiding unnecessary work when there are many cgroups."
Yes.

Let's read again:
"Working Set Detection

When a page is evicted, a compact shadow entry is stored in its place in the page cache or swap cache. The shadow records the eviction timestamp (in terms of the lruvec's nonresident age counter) and the cgroup and node that owned the page.

If the page is faulted back in (a "refault"), the shadow entry allows the kernel to compute the *refault distance* — how many other pages were activated or evicted between this page's eviction and its refault. If the refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list, the page was part of the active working set and is immediately activated rather than placed on the inactive list. This reduces thrashing by protecting frequently accessed pages that would otherwise be repeatedly evicted and refaulted."

Wait, could it be the inactive list in the case of Anon pages?
Let's look at `mm/workingset.c` line 512:
```c
	if (mem_cgroup_get_nr_swap_pages(eviction_memcg) > 0) {
		workingset_size += lruvec_page_state(eviction_lruvec,
						     NR_ACTIVE_ANON);
		if (file) {
			workingset_size += lruvec_page_state(eviction_lruvec,
						     NR_INACTIVE_ANON);
		}
	}
```
For file pages (if swap is available): `workingset_size = NR_ACTIVE_FILE + NR_ACTIVE_ANON + NR_INACTIVE_ANON`.
This is definitely NOT the "size of the inactive list". It's fundamentally the size of the active lists (the active working set) plus some anon.
The whole point is that we protect pages that would fit if memory was a bit larger (i.e. if the active list was slightly larger). The number of evictions represents the distance. If the distance is smaller than the active list size, it implies that the page could be kept in memory if it was considered part of the active list.
The bug is clear. The text incorrectly states that refault distance is compared to the size of the *inactive* list, while the actual implementation in `workingset_refault()` compares it to the size of the *active* lists (`NR_ACTIVE_FILE`, and optionally `NR_ACTIVE_ANON`/`NR_INACTIVE_ANON`), which represents the workingset size.

Are there any other bugs?
"The core reclaim loop (shrink_node()) divides its work between page cache / anonymous pages and slab caches. For each lruvec, it scans the inactive LRU lists, evaluating each page:"
"Clean file pages can be dropped immediately" -> they are unmapped or clean so they are dropped.
"Mapped pages require TLB invalidation (unmapping) before they can be freed. The rmap (reverse mapping) system is used to find and remove all page table entries pointing to the page." -> Wait, TLB invalidation is required when a mapped page is unmapped. But "unmapping before they can be freed" is true. Unmap involves TLB flush.

Let's look at `Scan Control`
`Each reclaim invocation is parameterized by a struct scan_control that captures the allocation context: which GFP flags were used, how many pages are needed, which node or memory cgroup to reclaim from, and whether writeback or swap are allowed. This struct threads through the entire reclaim stack, ensuring consistent policy at every level.`
This is true. `struct scan_control` has `gfp_mask`, `nr_to_reclaim`, `nodemask`, `target_mem_cgroup`, `may_writepage`, `may_swap`.

What about "Reclaim Priority"?
`The reclaim path operates at decreasing priority levels (from DEF_PRIORITY down to 0). At each level, a larger fraction of the LRU lists is scanned. At the default priority, only 1/4096th of pages are considered; at priority 0, the entire list is scanned.`

Let's check if $1/4096$ is correct.
The scanned size is calculated as `size >> priority`.
If priority is 12 (DEF_PRIORITY), it scans `size >> 12`, which is $1/4096$.
This is technically true.

Is there any missing API callbacks and interface omissions?
None, this commit only touches documentation.

Let's review the prompt again:
"Look for undocumented side-effects, missing pieces (e.g., a core change without updating corresponding callers, or changing a struct without updating all initializers), and unhandled corner cases related to the feature's logic."
"Verify that all claims in the commit message are fully realized in the code."
"Assume that the message might be incorrect or even intentionally malicious."

The main issue is that the text explains the working set detection algorithm wrong. It states that refault distance is compared to the size of the inactive list, but the code compares it to the size of the active list(s) (i.e. the workingset size). The workingset size is fundamentally based on `NR_ACTIVE_FILE` (plus anon memory depending on swap). This is a critical mathematical error in describing the workingset algorithm.

Another minor thing: "To prevent them from accumulating indefinitely, a shrinker reclaims shadow entries from page cache radix tree nodes that contain only shadows and no actual pages."
Wait, in `mm/workingset.c`, it actually reclaims from XArray nodes (`struct xa_node`), not radix tree nodes. Radix trees were replaced by XArray.
"page cache radix tree nodes" is technically outdated but people sometimes still call them that informally. But it's an inaccuracy.
Wait, `xa_node` is essentially the radix tree node, but the API and structure was renamed. I can mention it.

Let's search for "radix tree" in `mm/workingset.c`.

"If the refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list" -> "shorter than the size of the active list".

```json
{
  "concerns": [
    {
      "type": "Technical Inaccuracy",
      "description": "The workingset refault documentation incorrectly states that the refault distance is compared against the size of the inactive list.",
      "reasoning": "In the working set detection algorithm implemented in `mm/workingset.c`, the refault distance (number of evictions since the page was evicted) is compared against the size of the active lists (the workingset size, which includes `NR_ACTIVE_FILE` and potentially anon pages depending on swap availability), not the inactive list. If the distance is smaller than the active list size, it implies the page would have remained in the active working set if memory were slightly larger. Comparing it to the inactive list size is mathematically and conceptually incorrect."
    }
  ]
}

That means that each cgroup has an LRU\n\t * order that is composed of the LRU orders of its child\n\t * cgroups; and every page has an LRU position not just in the\n\t * cgroup that owns it, but in all of that group's ancestors.\n\t *\n\t * So when the physical inactive list of a leaf cgroup ages,\n\t * the virtual inactive lists of all its parents, including\n\t * the root cgroup's, age as well.\n\t */\n\tdo {\n\t\tatomic_long_add(nr_pages, &lruvec->nonresident_age);\n\t} while ((lruvec = parent_lruvec(lruvec)));\n}\n\n/**\n * workingset_eviction - note the eviction of a folio from memory\n * @target_memcg: the cgroup that is causing the reclaim\n * @folio: the folio being evicted\n *\n * Return: a shadow entry to be stored in @folio->mapping->i_pages in place\n * of the evicted @folio so that a later refault can be detected.\n */\nvoid *workingset_eviction(struct folio *folio, struct mem_cgroup *target_memcg)\n{\n\tstruct pglist_data *pgdat = folio_pgdat(folio);\n\tunsigned long eviction;\n\tstruct lruvec *lruvec;\n\tint memcgid;\n\n\t/* Folio is fully exclusive and pins folio's memory cgroup pointer */\n\tVM_BUG_ON_FOLIO(folio_test_lru(folio), folio);\n\tVM_BUG_ON_FOLIO(folio_ref_count(folio), folio);\n\tVM_BUG_ON_FOLIO(!folio_test_locked(folio), folio);\n\n\tif (lru_gen_enabled())\n\t\treturn lru_gen_eviction(folio);\n\n\tlruvec = mem_cgroup_lruvec(target_memcg, pgdat);\n\t/* XXX: target_memcg can be NULL, go through lruvec */\n\tmemcgid = mem_cgroup_private_id(lruvec_memcg(lruvec));\n\teviction = atomic_long_read(&lruvec->nonresident_age);\n\teviction >>= bucket_order;\n\tworkingset_age_nonresident(lruvec, folio_nr_pages(folio));\n\treturn pack_shadow(memcgid, pgdat, eviction,\n\t\t\t\tfolio_test_workingset(folio));\n}\n\n/**\n * workingset_test_recent - tests if the shadow entry is for a folio that was\n * recently evicted. Also fills in @workingset with the value unpacked from\n * shadow.\n * @shadow: the shadow entry to be tested.\n * @file: whether the corresponding folio is from the file lru.\n * @workingset: where the workingset value unpacked from shadow should\n * be stored.\n * @flush: whether to flush cgroup rstat.\n *\n * Return: true if the shadow is for a recently evicted folio; false otherwise.\n */\nbool workingset_test_recent(void *shadow, bool file, bool *workingset,\n\t\t\t\tbool flush)\n{\n\tstruct mem_cgroup *eviction_memcg;\n\tstruct lruvec *eviction_lruvec;\n\tunsigned long refault_distance;\n\tunsigned long workingset_size;\n\tunsigned long refault;\n\tint memcgid;\n\tstruct pglist_data *pgdat;\n\tunsigned long eviction;\n\n\tif (lru_gen_enabled()) {\n\t\tbool recent;\n\n\t\trcu_read_lock();\n\t\trecent = lru_gen_test_recent(shadow, &eviction_lruvec, &eviction, workingset);\n\t\trcu_read_unlock();\n\t\treturn recent;\n\t}\n\n\trcu_read_lock();\n\tunpack_shadow(shadow, &memcgid, &pgdat, &eviction, workingset);\n\teviction <<= bucket_order;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Look up the memcg associated with the stored ID. It might\n\t * have been deleted since the folio's eviction.\n\t *\n\t * Note that in rare events the ID could have been recycled\n\t * for a new cgroup that refaults a shared folio. This is\n\t * impossible to tell from the available data. However, this\n\t * should be a rare and limited disturbance, and activations\n\t * are always speculative anyway. Ultimately, it's the aging\n\t * algorithm's job to shake out the minimum access frequency\n\t * for the active cache.\n\t *\n\t * XXX: On !CONFIG_MEMCG, this will always return NULL; it\n\t * would be better if the root_mem_cgroup existed in all\n\t * configurations instead.\n\t */\n\teviction_memcg = mem_cgroup_from_private_id(memcgid);\n\tif (!mem_cgroup_tryget(eviction_memcg))\n\t\teviction_memcg = NULL;\n\trcu_read_unlock();\n\n\tif (!mem_cgroup_disabled() && !eviction_memcg)\n\t\treturn false;\n\t/*\n\t * Flush stats (and potentially sleep) outside the RCU read section.\n\t *\n\t * Note that workingset_test_recent() itself might be called in RCU read\n\t * section (for e.g, in cachestat) - these callers need to skip flushing\n\t * stats (via the flush argument).\n\t *\n\t * XXX: With per-memcg flushing and thresholding, is ratelimiting\n\t * still needed here?\n\t */\n\tif (flush)\n\t\tmem_cgroup_flush_stats_ratelimited(eviction_memcg);\n\n\teviction_lruvec = mem_cgroup_lruvec(eviction_memcg, pgdat);\n\trefault = atomic_long_read(&eviction_lruvec->nonresident_age);\n\n\t/*\n\t * Calculate the refault distance\n\t *\n\t * The unsigned subtraction here gives an accurate distance\n\t * across nonresident_age overflows in most cases. There is a\n\t * special case: usually, shadow entries have a short lifetime\n\t * and are either refaulted or reclaimed along with the inode\n\t * before they get too old.  But it is not impossible for the\n\t * nonresident_age to lap a shadow entry in the field, which\n\t * can then result in a false small refault distance, leading\n\t * to a false activation should this old entry actually\n\t * refault again.  However, earlier kernels used to deactivate\n\t * unconditionally with *every* reclaim invocation for the\n\t * longest time, so the occasional inappropriate activation\n\t * leading to pressure on the active list is not a problem.\n\t */\n\trefault_distance = (refault - eviction) & EVICTION_MASK;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Compare the distance to the existing workingset size. We\n\t * don't activate pages that couldn't stay resident even if\n\t * all the memory was available to the workingset. Whether\n\t * workingset competition needs to consider anon or not depends\n\t * on having free swap space.\n\t */\n\tworkingset_size = lruvec_page_state(eviction_lruvec, NR_ACTIVE_FILE);\n\tif (!file) {\n\t\tworkingset_size += lruvec_page_state(eviction_lruvec,\n\t\t\t\t\t\t     NR_INACTIVE_FILE);\n\t}\n\tif (mem_cgroup_get_nr_swap_pages(eviction_memcg) > 0) {\n\t\tworkingset_size += lruvec_page_state(eviction_lruvec,\n\t\t\t\t\t\t     NR_ACTIVE_ANON);\n\t\tif (file) {\n\t\t\tworkingset_size += lruvec_page_state(eviction_lruvec,\n\t\t\t\t\t\t     NR_INACTIVE_ANON);\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\tmem_cgroup_put(eviction_memcg);\n\treturn refault_distance <= workingset_size;\n}\n\n/**\n * workingset_refault - Evaluate the refault of a previously evicted folio.\n * @folio: The freshly allocated replacement folio.\n * @shadow: Shadow entry of the evicted folio.\n *\n * Calculates and evaluates the refault distance of the previously\n * evicted folio in the context of the node and the memcg whose memory\n * pressure caused the eviction.\n */\nvoid workingset_refault(struct folio *folio, void *shadow)\n{\n\tbool file = folio_is_file_lru(folio);\n\tstruct pglist_data *pgdat;\n\tstruct mem_cgroup *memcg;\n\tstruct lruvec *lruvec;\n\tbool workingset;\n\tlong nr;\n\n\tVM_BUG_ON_FOLIO(!folio_test_locked(folio), folio);\n\n\tif (lru_gen_enabled()) {\n\t\tlru_gen_refault(folio, shadow);\n\t\treturn;\n\t}\n\n\t/*\n\t * The activation decision for this folio is made at the level\n\t * where the eviction occurred, as that is where the LRU order\n\t * during folio reclaim is being determined.\n\t *\n\t * However, the cgroup that will own the folio is the one that\n\t * is actually experiencing the refault event. Make sure the folio is\n\t * locked to guarantee folio_memcg() stability throughout.\n\t */\n\tnr = folio_nr_pages(folio);\n\tmemcg = folio_memcg(folio);\n\tpgdat = folio_pgdat(folio);\n\tlruvec = mem_cgroup_lruvec(memcg, pgdat);\n\n\tmod_lruvec_state(lruvec, WORKINGSET_REFAULT_BASE + file, nr);\n\n\tif (!workingset_test_recent(shadow, file, &workingset, true))\n\t\treturn;\n\n\tfolio_set_active(folio);\n\tworkingset_age_nonresident(lruvec, nr);\n\tmod_lruvec_state(lruvec, WORKINGSET_ACTIVATE_BASE + file, nr);\n\n\t/* Folio was active prior to eviction */\n\tif (workingset) {\n\t\tfolio_set_workingset(folio);\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * XXX: Move to folio_add_lru() when it supports new vs\n\t\t * putback\n\t\t */\n\t\tlru_note_cost_refault(folio);\n\t\tmod_lruvec_state(lruvec, WORKINGSET_RESTORE_BASE + file, nr);\n\t}\n}\n\n/**\n * workingset_activation - note a page activation\n * @folio: Folio that is being activated.\n */\nvoid workingset_activation(struct folio *folio)\n{\n\t/*\n\t * Filter non-memcg pages here, e.g. unmap can call\n\t * mark_page_accessed() on VDSO pages.\n\t */\n\tif (mem_cgroup_disabled() || folio_memcg_charged(folio))\n\t\tworkingset_age_nonresident(folio_lruvec(folio), folio_nr_pages(folio));\n}\n\n/*\n * Shadow entries reflect the share of the working set that does not\n * fit into memory, so their number depends on the access pattern of\n * the workload.  In most cases, they will refault or get reclaimed\n * along with the inode, but a (malicious) workload that streams\n * through files with a total size several times that of available\n * memory, while preventing the inodes from being reclaimed, can\n * create excessive amounts of shadow nodes.  To keep a lid on this,\n * track shadow nodes and reclaim them when they grow way past the\n * point where they would still be useful.\n */\n\nstruct list_lru shadow_nodes;\n\nvoid workingset_update_node(struct xa_node *node)\n{\n\tstruct page *page = virt_to_page(node);\n\n\t/*\n\t * Track non-empty nodes that contain only shadow entries;\n\t * unlink those that contain pages or are being freed.\n\t *\n\t * Avoid acquiring the list_lru lock when the nodes are\n\t * already where they should be. The list_empty() test is safe\n\t * as node->private_list is protected by the i_pages lock.\n\t */\n\tlockdep_assert_held(&node->array->xa_lock);\n\n\tif (node->count && node->count == node->nr_values) {\n\t\tif (list_empty(&node->private_list)) {\n\t\t\tlist_lru_add_obj(&shadow_nodes, &node->private_list);\n\t\t\t__inc_node_page_state(page, WORKINGSET_NODES);\n\t\t}\n\t} else {\n\t\tif (!list_empty(&node->private_list)) {\n\t\t\tlist_lru_del_obj(&shadow_nodes, &node->private_list);\n\t\t\t__dec_node_page_state(page, WORKINGSET_NODES);\n\t\t}\n\t}\n}\n\nstatic unsigned long count_shadow_nodes(struct shrinker *shrinker,\n\t\t\t\t\tstruct shrink_control *sc)\n{\n\tunsigned long max_nodes;\n\tunsigned long nodes;\n\tunsigned long pages;\n\n\tnodes = list_lru_shrink_count(&shadow_nodes, sc);\n\tif (!nodes)\n\t\treturn SHRINK_EMPTY;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Approximate a reasonable limit for the nodes\n\t * containing shadow entries. We don't need to keep more\n\t * shadow entries than possible pages on the active list,\n\t * since refault distances bigger than that are dismissed.\n\t *\n\t * The size of the active list converges toward 100% of\n\t * overall page cache as memory grows, with only a tiny\n\t * inactive list. Assume the total cache size for that.\n\t *\n\t * Nodes might be sparsely populated, with only one shadow\n\t * entry in the extreme case. Obviously, we cannot keep one\n\t * node for every eligible shadow entry, so compromise on a\n\t * worst-case density of 1/8th. Below that, not all eligible\n\t * refaults can be detected anymore.\n\t *\n\t * On 64-bit with 7 xa_nodes per page and 64 slots\n\t * each, this will reclaim shadow entries when they consume\n\t * ~1.8% of available memory:\n\t *\n\t * PAGE_SIZE / xa_nodes / node_entries * 8 / PAGE_SIZE\n\t */\n#ifdef CONFIG_MEMCG\n\tif (sc->memcg) {\n\t\tstruct lruvec *lruvec;\n\t\tint i;\n\n\t\tmem_cgroup_flush_stats_ratelimited(sc->memcg);\n\t\tlruvec = mem_cgroup_lruvec(sc->memcg, NODE_DATA(sc->nid));\n\t\tfor (pages = 0, i = 0; i < NR_LRU_LISTS; i++)\n\t\t\tpages += lruvec_page_state_local(lruvec,\n\t\t\t\t\t\t\t NR_LRU_BASE + i);\n\t\tpages += lruvec_page_state_local(\n\t\t\tlruvec, NR_SLAB_RECLAIMABLE_B) >> PAGE_SHIFT;\n\t\tpages += lruvec_page_state_local(\n\t\t\tlruvec, NR_SLAB_UNRECLAIMABLE_B) >> PAGE_SHIFT;\n\t} else\n#endif\n\t\tpages = node_present_pages(sc->nid);\n\n\tmax_nodes = pages >> (XA_CHUNK_SHIFT - 3);\n\n\tif (nodes <= max_nodes)\n\t\treturn 0;\n\treturn nodes - max_nodes;\n}\n\nstatic enum lru_status shadow_lru_isolate(struct list_head *item,\n\t\t\t\t\t  struct list_lru_one *lru,\n\t\t\t\t\t  void *arg) __must_hold(lru->lock)\n{\n\tstruct xa_node *node = container_of(item, struct xa_node, private_list);\n\tstruct address_space *mapping;\n\tint ret;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Page cache insertions and deletions synchronously maintain\n\t * the shadow node LRU under the i_pages lock and the\n\t * &lru->lock. Because the page cache tree is emptied before\n\t * the inode can be destroyed, holding the &lru->lock pins any\n\t * address_space that has nodes on the LRU.\n\t *\n\t * We can then safely transition to the i_pages lock to\n\t * pin only the address_space of the particular node we want\n\t * to reclaim, take the node off-LRU, and drop the &lru->lock.\n\t */\n\n\tmapping = container_of(node->array, struct address_space, i_pages);\n\n\t/* Coming from the list, invert the lock order */\n\tif (!xa_trylock(&mapping->i_pages)) {\n\t\tspin_unlock_irq(&lru->lock);\n\t\tret = LRU_RETRY;\n\t\tgoto out;\n\t}\n\n\t/* For page cache we need to hold i_lock */\n\tif (mapping->host != NULL) {\n\t\tif (!spin_trylock(&mapping->host->i_lock)) {\n\t\t\txa_unlock(&mapping->i_pages);\n\t\t\tspin_unlock_irq(&lru->lock);\n\t\t\tret = LRU_RETRY;\n\t\t\tgoto out;\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\tlist_lru_isolate(lru, item);\n\t__dec_node_page_state(virt_to_page(node), WORKINGSET_NODES);\n\n\tspin_unlock(&lru->lock);\n\n\t/*\n\t * The nodes should only contain one or more shadow entries,\n\t * no pages, so we expect to be able to remove them all and\n\t * delete and free the empty node afterwards.\n\t */\n\tif (WARN_ON_ONCE(!node->nr_values))\n\t\tgoto out_invalid;\n\tif (WARN_ON_ONCE(node->count != node->nr_values))\n\t\tgoto out_invalid;\n\txa_delete_node(node, workingset_update_node);\n\tmod_lruvec_kmem_state(node, WORKINGSET_NODERECLAIM, 1);\n\nout_invalid:\n\txa_unlock_irq(&mapping->i_pages);\n\tif (mapping->host != NULL) {\n\t\tif (mapping_shrinkable(mapping))\n\t\t\tinode_lru_list_add(mapping->host);\n\t\tspin_unlock(&mapping->host->i_lock);\n\t}\n\tret = LRU_REMOVED_RETRY;\nout:\n\tcond_resched();\n\treturn ret;\n}\n\nstatic unsigned long scan_shadow_nodes(struct shrinker *shrinker,\n\t\t\t\t       struct shrink_control *sc)\n{\n\t/* list_lru lock nests inside the IRQ-safe i_pages lock */\n\treturn list_lru_shrink_walk_irq(&shadow_nodes, sc, shadow_lru_isolate,\n\t\t\t\t\tNULL);\n}\n\n/*\n * Our list_lru->lock is IRQ-safe as it nests inside the IRQ-safe\n * i_pages lock.\n */\nstatic struct lock_class_key shadow_nodes_key;\n\nstatic int __init workingset_init(void)\n{\n\tstruct shrinker *workingset_shadow_shrinker;\n\tunsigned int timestamp_bits;\n\tunsigned int max_order;\n\tint ret = -ENOMEM;\n\n\tBUILD_BUG_ON(BITS_PER_LONG < EVICTION_SHIFT);\n\t/*\n\t * Calculate the eviction bucket size to cover the longest\n\t * actionable refault distance, which is currently half of\n\t * memory (totalram_pages/2). However, memory hotplug may add\n\t * some more pages at runtime, so keep working with up to\n\t * double the initial memory by using totalram_pages as-is.\n\t */\n\ttimestamp_bits = BITS_PER_LONG - EVICTION_SHIFT;\n\tmax_order = fls_long(totalram_pages() - 1);\n\tif (max_order > timestamp_bits)\n\t\tbucket_order = max_order - timestamp_bits;\n\tpr_info(\"workingset: timestamp_bits=%d max_order=%d bucket_order=%u\\n\",\n\t       timestamp_bits, max_order, bucket_order);\n\n\tworkingset_shadow_shrinker = shrinker_alloc(SHRINKER_NUMA_AWARE |\n\t\t\t\t\t\t    SHRINKER_MEMCG_AWARE,\n\t\t\t\t\t\t    \"mm-shadow\");\n\tif (!workingset_shadow_shrinker)\n\t\tgoto err;\n\n\tret = list_lru_init_memcg_key(&shadow_nodes, workingset_shadow_shrinker,\n\t\t\t\t      &shadow_nodes_key);\n\tif (ret)\n\t\tgoto err_list_lru;\n\n\tworkingset_shadow_shrinker->count_objects = count_shadow_nodes;\n\tworkingset_shadow_shrinker->scan_objects = scan_shadow_nodes;\n\t/* ->count reports only fully expendable nodes */\n\tworkingset_shadow_shrinker->seeks = 0;\n\n\tshrinker_register(workingset_shadow_shrinker);\n\treturn 0;\nerr_list_lru:\n\tshrinker_free(workingset_shadow_shrinker);\nerr:\n\treturn ret;\n}\nmodule_init(workingset_init);","end_line":826,"start_line":332,"total_lines":826}",

    "thought": "**Analyzing the Working Set Refault Logic**

Okay, diving into the `workingset_test_recent()` function, I'm trying to wrap my head around this workingset size calculation. I see it's taking the sizes of different LRU lists—`NR_ACTIVE_FILE`, `NR_INACTIVE_FILE`, `NR_ACTIVE_ANON`, and `NR_INACTIVE_ANON`—and adding them up based on whether it's a file page or an anonymous page and if swap is in use. The comparison hinges on `refault_distance <= workingset_size`.

Initially, it appears the workingset size calculation aims to capture something about the "active working set." If it is for a file page, then `workingset_size` comprises `NR_ACTIVE_FILE`, `NR_ACTIVE_ANON`, and `NR_INACTIVE_ANON`. However, for an anonymous page, it includes `NR_ACTIVE_FILE`, `NR_INACTIVE_FILE`, and `NR_ACTIVE_ANON`. Therefore, the "workingset size" effectively represents the sum of the active lists (file and anon) PLUS the inactive list of the other type. In other words, this sum excludes the inactive list of the same type.

This calculation is the crux of understanding the refault logic. The code itself is straightforward, but its relation to the high-level explanation in the documentation is confusing.

Now, here's where it gets interesting and where the documentation seems to be off. The documentation says: "If the refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list, the page was part of the active working set and is immediately activated rather than placed on the inactive list." That doesn't align with my understanding of `workingset_size`. The `workingset_size` is calculated excluding the inactive list of the same type and is, therefore, more like the size of the active set.

So, I think the comparison of `refault_distance <= workingset_size` translates to: If the refault distance (number of evictions since this page was evicted) is shorter than the *active* working set (calculated as all the memory excluding the inactive list of the same type), the page would have survived in memory had it been in the active list.

Looking back to Johannes' original commit, the `refault_distance` is the number of evictions since this page was evicted. If that distance is shorter than the *active list* (or the workingset size, which I understand is all of memory excluding the inactive list of the same type), then the page would have remained in the active list. So, it's activated, which makes perfect sense.

My interpretation, the documentation's phrasing regarding the *inactive* list, is inaccurate. The distance is compared to the active list, which is the exact opposite of what the documentation states. If a page refaults with distance `D` and `D` < `size of active list`, it means it would still be in the active list, and it gets activated.

To be absolutely certain, I'm double-checking the code and documentation to confirm that the `workingset_size` and the comparison are about the active list size. I see that the kernel documentation says, "The refault distance is compared to the size of the active list." Therefore, if the `refault_distance <= active_list`, the page is activated, because it would have survived.

Finally, `mm/workingset.c` confirms this: "If the refault distance is smaller than the active list, we activate the page." The documentation's "inactive list" phrasing is a factual error. It should be "active list" (or "active working set"). That makes the whole algorithm crystal clear!

    "content": "{"output":"commit 4d5d14a01e2c9091b128fb46e1d07475e9a7bb72\nAuthor: Yu Zhao <yuzhao@google.com>\nDate:   Mon Dec 30 21:35:37 2024 -0700\n\n    mm/mglru: rework workingset protection\n    \n    With the aging feedback no longer considering the distribution of folios\n    in each generation, rework workingset protection to better distribute\n    folios across MAX_NR_GENS.  This is achieved by reusing PG_workingset and\n    PG_referenced/LRU_REFS_FLAGS in a slightly different way.\n    \n    For folios accessed multiple times through file descriptors, make\n    lru_gen_inc_refs() set additional bits of LRU_REFS_WIDTH in folio->flags\n    after PG_referenced, then PG_workingset after LRU_REFS_WIDTH.  After all\n    its bits are set, i.e., LRU_REFS_FLAGS|BIT(PG_workingset), a folio is\n    lazily promoted into the second oldest generation in the eviction path.\n    And when folio_inc_gen() does that, it clears LRU_REFS_FLAGS so that\n    lru_gen_inc_refs() can start over.  For this case, LRU_REFS_MASK is only\n    valid when PG_referenced is set.\n    \n    For folios accessed multiple times through page tables, folio_update_gen()\n    from a page table walk or lru_gen_set_refs() from a rmap walk sets\n    PG_referenced after the accessed bit is cleared for the first time.\n    Thereafter, those two paths set PG_workingset and promote folios to the\n    youngest generation.  Like folio_inc_gen(), when folio_update_gen() does\n    that, it also clears PG_referenced.  For this case, LRU_REFS_MASK is not\n    used.\n    \n    For both of the cases, after PG_workingset is set on a folio, it remains\n    until this folio is either reclaimed, or \"deactivated\" by\n    lru_gen_clear_refs().  It can be set again if lru_gen_test_recent()\n    returns true upon a refault.\n    \n    When adding folios to the LRU lists, lru_gen_folio_seq() distributes\n    them as follows:\n    +---------------------------------+---------------------------------+\n    |    Accessed thru page tables    | Accessed thru file descriptors  |\n    +---------------------------------+---------------------------------+\n    | PG_active (set while isolated)  |                                 |\n    +----------------+----------------+----------------+----------------+\n    | PG_workingset  | PG_referenced  | PG_workingset  | LRU_REFS_FLAGS |\n    +---------------------------------+---------------------------------+\n    |<--------- MIN_NR_GENS --------->|                                 |\n    |<-------------------------- MAX_NR_GENS -------------------------->|\n    \n    After this patch, some typical client and server workloads showed\n    improvements under heavy memory pressure.  For example, Python TPC-C,\n    which was used to benchmark a different approach [1] to better detect\n    refault distances, showed a significant decrease in total refaults:\n    \n                                Before      After      Change\n      Time (seconds)            10801       10801      0%\n      Executed (transactions)   41472       43663      +5%\n      workingset_nodes          109070      120244     +10%\n      workingset_refault_anon   5019627     7281831    +45%\n      workingset_refault_file   1294678786  554855564  -57%\n      workingset_refault_total  1299698413  562137395  -57%\n    \n    [1] https://lore.kernel.org/20230920190244.16839-1-ryncsn@gmail.com/\n    \n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20241231043538.4075764-7-yuzhao@google.com\n    Signed-off-by: Yu Zhao <yuzhao@google.com>\n    Reported-by: Kairui Song <kasong@tencent.com>\n    Closes: https://lore.kernel.org/CAOUHufahuWcKf5f1Sg3emnqX+cODuR=2TQo7T4Gr-QYLujn4RA@mail.gmail.com/\n    Tested-by: Kalesh Singh <kaleshsingh@google.com>\n    Cc: Barry Song <v-songbaohua@oppo.com>\n    Cc: Bharata B Rao <bharata@amd.com>\n    Cc: David Stevens <stevensd@chromium.org>\n    Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>\n"}",

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analyzing the Memory Management Documentation**

Okay, let's break this down. First, regarding the active vs. inactive list logic: the commit's description has a clear factual error. It currently says "if the refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list," but that's wrong. It *should* be the *active* list. If the refault distance is smaller than the active list's size, it means that during the page's absence, fewer pages were activated than the active list's capacity. Therefore, if the page had been placed at the head of the active list when it was evicted, it would still be in the active list now. The current documentation is definitely wrong on this point; it misidentifies the list. I'm going to flag that.

Let me just double check, `mm/workingset.c` confirms this: It calculates the *working set* size, which is essentially the active list. So, using the size of the *inactive* list there is a technical error.

Next, regarding "Mapped pages" and TLB invalidation, that's accurate as a high-level summary. I'm okay with that.

Now, concerning the LRU scanning: the documentation states that at the default priority, only 1/4096th of pages are considered, and at priority 0, the entire list is scanned. I need to make sure this is accurate. Priority 12 leads to `1 >> 12`, so that's the equivalent of `1/4096`. Priority 0 is `total_size >> 0` which is just `total_size`, the entire list. That seems correct. I'll just keep an eye on this when implementing it.

Finally, I need to check the multi-gen LRU batching. The text describes how LRU operations are batched per-CPU to avoid locking overhead. This seems correct generally, and the general description is correct:  `folio_add_lru()` and `folio_mark_accessed()` *queue* pages into per-CPU folio batches. However, upon closer inspection, `folio_mark_accessed()` doesn't queue pages directly. It updates `PG_referenced` and may call `folio_activate()`, which *does* queue the pages into `cpu_fbatches.lru_activate`. I need to be careful to remember that *difference* when working with the implementation.

But,\n\t\t * this list is never rotated or maintained, so marking an\n\t\t * unevictable page accessed has no effect.\n\t\t */\n\t} else if (!folio_test_active(folio)) {\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * If the folio is on the LRU, queue it for activation via\n\t\t * cpu_fbatches.lru_activate. Otherwise, assume the folio is in a\n\t\t * folio_batch, mark it active and it'll be moved to the active\n\t\t * LRU on the next drain.\n\t\t */\n\t\tif (folio_test_lru(folio))\n\t\t\tfolio_activate(folio);\n\t\telse\n\t\t\t__lru_cache_activate_folio(folio);\n\t\tfolio_clear_referenced(folio);\n\t\tworkingset_activation(folio);\n\t}\n\tif (folio_test_idle(folio))\n\t\tfolio_clear_idle(folio);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(folio_mark_accessed);\n\n/**\n * folio_add_lru - Add a folio to an LRU list.\n * @folio: The folio to be added to the LRU.\n *\n * Queue the folio for addition to the LRU. The decision on whether\n * to add the page to the [in]active [file|anon] list is deferred until the\n * folio_batch is drained. This gives a chance for the caller of folio_add_lru()\n * have the folio added to the active list using folio_mark_accessed().\n */\nvoid folio_add_lru(struct folio *folio)\n{\n\tVM_BUG_ON_FOLIO(folio_test_active(folio) &&\n\t\t\tfolio_test_unevictable(folio), folio);\n\tVM_BUG_ON_FOLIO(folio_test_lru(folio), folio);\n\n\t/* see the comment in lru_gen_folio_seq() */\n\tif (lru_gen_enabled() && !folio_test_unevictable(folio) &&\n\t    lru_gen_in_fault() && !(current->flags & PF_MEMALLOC))\n\t\tfolio_set_active(folio);\n\n\tfolio_batch_add_and_move(folio, lru_add);\n}\nEXPORT_SYMBOL(folio_add_lru);\n\n/**\n * folio_add_lru_vma() - Add a folio to the appropriate LRU list for this VMA.\n * @folio: The folio to be added to the LRU.\n * @vma: VMA in which the folio is mapped.\n *\n * If the VMA is mlocked, @folio is added to the unevictable list.\n * Otherwise, it is treated the same way as folio_add_lru().\n */\nvoid folio_add_lru_vma(struct folio *folio, struct vm_area_struct *vma)\n{\n\tVM_BUG_ON_FOLIO(folio_test_lru(folio), folio);\n\n\tif (unlikely((vma->vm_flags & (VM_LOCKED | VM_SPECIAL)) == VM_LOCKED))\n\t\tmlock_new_folio(folio);\n\telse\n\t\tfolio_add_lru(folio);\n}\n\n/*\n * If the folio cannot be invalidated, it is moved to the\n * inactive list to speed up its reclaim.  It is moved to the\n * head of the list, rather than the tail, to give the flusher\n * threads some time to write it out, as this is much more\n * effective than the single-page writeout from reclaim.\n *\n * If the folio isn't mapped and dirty/writeback, the folio\n * could be reclaimed asap using the reclaim flag.\n *\n * 1. active, mapped folio -> none\n * 2. active, dirty/writeback folio -> inactive, head, reclaim\n * 3. inactive, mapped folio -> none\n * 4. inactive, dirty/writeback folio -> inactive, head, reclaim\n * 5. inactive, clean -> inactive, tail\n * 6. Others -> none\n *\n * In 4, it moves to the head of the inactive list so the folio is\n * written out by flusher threads as this is much more efficient\n * than the single-page writeout from reclaim.\n */\nstatic void lru_deactivate_file(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct folio *folio)\n{\n\tbool active = folio_test_active(folio) || lru_gen_enabled();\n\tlong nr_pages = folio_nr_pages(folio);\n\n\tif (folio_test_unevictable(folio))\n\t\treturn;\n\n\t/* Some processes are using the folio */\n\tif (folio_mapped(folio))\n\t\treturn;\n\n\tlruvec_del_folio(lruvec, folio);\n\tfolio_clear_active(folio);\n\tfolio_clear_referenced(folio);\n\n\tif (folio_test_writeback(folio) || folio_test_dirty(folio)) {\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * Setting the reclaim flag could race with\n\t\t * folio_end_writeback() and confuse readahead.  But the\n\t\t * race window is _really_ small and  it's not a critical\n\t\t * problem.\n\t\t */\n\t\tlruvec_add_folio(lruvec, folio);\n\t\tfolio_set_reclaim(folio);\n\t} else {\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * The folio's writeback ended while it was in the batch.\n\t\t * We move that folio to the tail of the inactive list.\n\t\t */\n\t\tlruvec_add_folio_tail(lruvec, folio);\n\t\t__count_vm_events(PGROTATED, nr_pages);\n\t}\n\n\tif (active) {\n\t\t__count_vm_events(PGDEACTIVATE, nr_pages);\n\t\tcount_memcg_events(lruvec_memcg(lruvec), PGDEACTIVATE,\n\t\t\t\t     nr_pages);\n\t}\n}\n\nstatic void lru_deactivate(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct folio *folio)\n{\n\tlong nr_pages = folio_nr_pages(folio);\n\n\tif (folio_test_unevictable(folio) || !(folio_test_active(folio) || lru_gen_enabled()))\n\t\treturn;\n\n\tlruvec_del_folio(lruvec, folio);\n\tfolio_clear_active(folio);\n\tfolio_clear_referenced(folio);\n\tlruvec_add_folio(lruvec, folio);\n\n\t__count_vm_events(PGDEACTIVATE, nr_pages);\n\tcount_memcg_events(lruvec_memcg(lruvec), PGDEACTIVATE, nr_pages);\n}\n\nstatic void lru_lazyfree(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct folio *folio)\n{\n\tlong nr_pages = folio_nr_pages(folio);\n\n\tif (!folio_test_anon(folio) || !folio_test_swapbacked(folio) ||\n\t    folio_test_swapcache(folio) || folio_test_unevictable(folio))\n\t\treturn;\n\n\tlruvec_del_folio(lruvec, folio);\n\tfolio_clear_active(folio);\n\tif (lru_gen_enabled())\n\t\tlru_gen_clear_refs(folio);\n\telse\n\t\tfolio_clear_referenced(folio);\n\t/*\n\t * Lazyfree folios are clean anonymous folios.  They have\n\t * the swapbacked flag cleared, to distinguish them from normal\n\t * anonymous folios\n\t */\n\tfolio_clear_swapbacked(folio);\n\tlruvec_add_folio(lruvec, folio);\n\n\t__count_vm_events(PGLAZYFREE, nr_pages);\n\tcount_memcg_events(lruvec_memcg(lruvec), PGLAZYFREE, nr_pages);\n}\n\n/*\n * Drain pages out of the cpu's folio_batch.\n * Either \"cpu\" is the current CPU, and preemption has already been\n * disabled; or \"cpu\" is being hot-unplugged, and is already dead.\n */\nvoid lru_add_drain_cpu(int cpu)\n{\n\tstruct cpu_fbatches *fbatches = &per_cpu(cpu_fbatches, cpu);\n\tstruct folio_batch *fbatch = &fbatches->lru_add;\n\n\tif (folio_batch_count(fbatch))\n\t\tfolio_batch_move_lru(fbatch, lru_add);\n\n\tfbatch = &fbatches->lru_move_tail;\n\t/* Disabling interrupts below acts as a compiler barrier. */\n\tif (data_race(folio_batch_count(fbatch))) {\n\t\tunsigned long flags;\n\n\t\t/* No harm done if a racing interrupt already did this */\n\t\tlocal_lock_irqsave(&cpu_fbatches.lock_irq, flags);\n\t\tfolio_batch_move_lru(fbatch, lru_move_tail);\n\t\tlocal_unlock_irqrestore(&cpu_fbatches.lock_irq, flags);\n\t}\n\n\tfbatch = &fbatches->lru_deactivate_file;\n\tif (folio_batch_count(fbatch))\n\t\tfolio_batch_move_lru(fbatch, lru_deactivate_file);\n\n\tfbatch = &fbatches->lru_deactivate;\n\tif (folio_batch_count(fbatch))\n\t\tfolio_batch_move_lru(fbatch, lru_deactivate);\n\n\tfbatch = &fbatches->lru_lazyfree;\n\tif (folio_batch_count(fbatch))\n\t\tfolio_batch_move_lru(fbatch, lru_lazyfree);\n\n\tfolio_activate_drain(cpu);\n}\n\n/**\n * deactivate_file_folio() - Deactivate a file folio.\n * @folio: Folio to deactivate.\n *\n * This function hints to the VM that @folio is a good reclaim candidate,\n * for example if its invalidation fails due to the folio being dirty\n * or under writeback.\n *\n * Context: Caller holds a reference on the folio.\n */\nvoid deactivate_file_folio(struct folio *folio)\n{\n\t/* Deactivating an unevictable folio will not accelerate reclaim */\n\tif (folio_test_unevictable(folio) || !folio_test_lru(folio))\n\t\treturn;\n\n\tif (lru_gen_enabled() && lru_gen_clear_refs(folio))\n\t\treturn;\n\n\tfolio_batch_add_and_move(folio, lru_deactivate_file);\n}\n\n/*\n * folio_deactivate - deactivate a folio\n * @folio: folio to deactivate\n *\n * folio_deactivate() moves @folio to the inactive list if @folio was on the\n * active list and was not unevictable. It shouldn't be a problem in !SMP case since\n * the core is only one and the locks will disable preemption.\n */\nstatic void lru_add_and_bh_lrus_drain(void)\n{\n\tlocal_lock(&cpu_fbatches.lock);\n\tlru_add_drain_cpu(smp_processor_id());\n\tlocal_unlock(&cpu_fbatches.lock);\n\tinvalidate_bh_lrus_cpu();\n\tmlock_drain_local();\n}\n\nvoid lru_add_drain_cpu_zone(struct zone *zone)\n{\n\tlocal_lock(&cpu_fbatches.lock);\n\tlru_add_drain_cpu(smp_processor_id());\n\tdrain_local_pages(zone);\n\tlocal_unlock(&cpu_fbatches.lock);\n\tmlock_drain_local();\n}\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_SMP\n\nstatic DEFINE_PER_CPU(struct work_struct, lru_add_drain_work);\n\nstatic void lru_add_drain_per_cpu(struct work_struct *dummy)\n{\n\tlru_add_and_bh_lrus_drain();\n}\n\nstatic bool cpu_needs_drain(unsigned int cpu)\n{\n\tstruct cpu_fbatches *fbatches = &per_cpu(cpu_fbatches, cpu);\n\n\t/* Check these in order of likelihood that they're not zero */\n\treturn folio_batch_count(&fbatches->lru_add) ||\n\t\tfolio_batch_count(&fbatches->lru_move_tail) ||\n\t\tfolio_batch_count(&fbatches->lru_deactivate_file) ||\n\t\tfolio_batch_count(&fbatches->lru_deactivate) ||\n\t\tfolio_batch_count(&fbatches->lru_lazyfree) ||\n\t\tfolio_batch_count(&fbatches->lru_activate) ||\n\t\tneed_mlock_drain(cpu) ||\n\t\thas_bh_in_lru(cpu, NULL);\n}\n\n/*\n * Doesn't need any cpu hotplug locking because we do rely on per-cpu\n * kworkers being shut down before our page_alloc_cpu_dead callback is\n * executed on the offlined cpu.\n * Calling this function with cpu hotplug locks held can actually lead\n * to obscure indirect dependencies via WQ context.\n */\nstatic inline void __lru_add_drain_all(bool force_all_cpus)\n{\n\t/*\n\t * lru_drain_gen - Global pages generation number\n\t *\n\t * (A) Definition: global lru_drain_gen = x implies that all generations\n\t *     0 < n <= x are already *scheduled* for draining.\n\t *\n\t * This is an optimization for the highly-contended use case where a\n\t * user space workload keeps constantly generating a flow of pages for\n\t * each CPU.\n\t */\n\tstatic unsigned int lru_drain_gen;\n\tstatic struct cpumask has_work;\n\tstatic DEFINE_MUTEX(lock);\n\tunsigned cpu, this_gen;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Make sure nobody triggers this path before mm_percpu_wq is fully\n\t * initialized.\n\t */\n\tif (WARN_ON(!mm_percpu_wq))\n\t\treturn;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Guarantee folio_batch counter stores visible by this CPU\n\t * are visible to other CPUs before loading the current drain\n\t * generation.\n\t */\n\tsmp_mb();\n\n\t/*\n\t * (B) Locally cache global LRU draining generation number\n\t *\n\t * The read barrier ensures that the counter is loaded before the mutex\n\t * is taken. Check (A).\n\t */\n\tif (unlikely(this_gen != lru_drain_gen && !force_all_cpus))\n\t\tgoto done;\n\n\t/*\n\t * (D) Increment global generation number\n\t *\n\t * Pairs with smp_load_acquire() at (B), outside of the critical\n\t * section. Use a full memory barrier to guarantee that the\n\t * new global drain generation number is stored before loading\n\t * folio_batch counters.\n\t *\n\t * This pairing must be done here, before the for_each_online_cpu loop\n\t * below which drains the page vectors.\n\t *\n\t * Let x, y, and z represent some system CPU numbers, where x < y < z.\n\t * Assume CPU #z is in the middle of the for_each_online_cpu loop\n\t * below and has already reached CPU #y's per-cpu data. CPU #x comes\n\t * along, adds some pages to its per-cpu vectors, then calls\n\t * lru_add_drain_all().\n\t *\n\t * If the paired barrier is done at any later step, e.g. after the\n\t * loop, CPU #x will just exit at (C) and miss flushing out all of its\n\t * added pages.\n\t */\n\tWRITE_ONCE(lru_drain_gen, lru_drain_gen + 1);\n\tsmp_mb();\n\n\tcpumask_clear(&has_work);\n\tfor_each_online_cpu(cpu) {\n\t\tstruct work_struct *work = &per_cpu(lru_add_drain_work, cpu);\n\n\t\tif (cpu_needs_drain(cpu)) {\n\t\t\tINIT_WORK(work, lru_add_drain_per_cpu);\n\t\t\tqueue_work_on(cpu, mm_percpu_wq, work);\n\t\t\t__cpumask_set_cpu(cpu, &has_work);\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\tfor_each_cpu(cpu, &has_work)\n\t\tflush_work(&per_cpu(lru_add_drain_work, cpu));\n\ndone:\n\tmutex_unlock(&lock);\n}\n\nvoid lru_add_drain_all(void)\n{\n\t__lru_add_drain_all(false);\n}\n#else\nvoid lru_add_drain_all(void)\n{\n\tlru_add_drain();\n}\n#endif /* CONFIG_SMP */\n\natomic_t lru_disable_count = ATOMIC_INIT(0);\n\n/*\n * lru_cache_disable() needs to be called before we start compiling\n * a list of folios to be migrated using folio_isolate_lru().\n * It drains folios on LRU cache and then disable on all cpus until\n * lru_cache_enable is called.\n *\n * Must be paired with a call to lru_cache_enable().\n */\nvoid lru_cache_disable(void)\n{\n\tatomic_inc(&lru_disable_count);\n\t/*\n\t * Readers of lru_disable_count are protected by either disabling\n\t * preemption or rcu_read_lock:\n\t *\n\t * preempt_disable, local_irq_disable  [bh_lru_lock()]\n\t * rcu_read_lock\t\t       [rt_spin_lock CONFIG_PREEMPT_RT]\n\t * preempt_disable\t\t       [local_lock !CONFIG_PREEMPT_RT]\n\t *\n\t * Since v5.1 kernel, synchronize_rcu() is guaranteed to wait on\n\t * preempt_disable() regions of code. So any CPU which sees\n\t * lru_disable_count = 0 will have exited the critical\n\t * section when synchronize_rcu() returns.\n\t */\n\tsynchronize_rcu_expedited();\n#ifdef CONFIG_SMP\n\t__lru_add_drain_all(true);\n#else\n\tlru_add_and_bh_lrus_drain();\n#endif\n}\n\n/**\n * folios_put_refs - Reduce the reference count on a batch of folios.\n * @folios: The folios.\n * @refs: The number of refs to subtract from each folio.\n *\n * Like folio_put(), but for a batch of folios.  This is more efficient\n * than writing the loop yourself as it will optimise the locks which need\n * to be taken if the folios are freed.  The folios batch is returned\n * empty and ready to be reused for another batch; there is no need\n * to reinitialise it.  If @refs is NULL, we subtract one from each\n * folio refcount.\n *\n * Context: May be called in process or interrupt context, but not in NMI\n * context. Freshly faulted pages start out at\n * the head of the inactive list and page reclaim scans pages from the\n * tail.  Pages that are accessed multiple times on the inactive list\n * are promoted to the active list, to protect them from reclaim,\n * whereas active pages are demoted to the inactive list when the\n * active list grows too big.\n *\n *   fault ------------------------+\n *                                 |\n *              +--------------+   |            +-------------+\n *   reclaim <- |   inactive   | <-+-- demotion |    active   | <--+\n *              +--------------+                +-------------+    |\n *                     |                                           |\n *                     +-------------- promotion ------------------+\n *\n *\n *\t\tAccess frequency and refault distance\n *\n * A workload is thrashing when its pages are frequently used but they\n * are evicted from the inactive list every time before another access\n * would have promoted them to the active list.\n *\n * In cases where the average access distance between thrashing pages\n * is bigger than the size of memory there is nothing that can be\n * done - the thrashing set could never fit into memory under any\n * circumstance.\n *\n * However, the average access distance could be bigger than the\n * inactive list, yet smaller than the size of memory.  In this case,\n * the set could fit into memory if it weren't for the currently\n * active pages - which may be used more, hopefully less frequently:\n *\n *      +-memory available to cache-+\n *      |                           |\n *      +-inactive------+-active----+\n *  a b | c d e f g h i | J K L M N |\n *      +---------------+-----------+\n *\n * It is prohibitively expensive to accurately track access frequency\n * of pages.  But a reasonable approximation can be made to measure\n * thrashing on the inactive list, after which refaulting pages can be\n * activated optimistically to compete with the existing active pages.\n *\n * Approximating inactive page access frequency - Observations:\n *\n * 1. When a page is accessed for the first time, it is added to the\n *    head of the inactive list, slides every existing inactive page\n *    towards the tail by one slot, and pushes the current tail page\n *    out of memory.\n *\n * 2. When a page is accessed for the second time, it is promoted to\n *    the active list, shrinking the inactive list by one slot.  This\n *    also slides all inactive pages that were faulted into the cache\n *    more recently than the activated page towards the tail of the\n *    inactive list.\n *\n * Thus:\n *\n * 1. The sum of evictions and activations between any two points in\n *    time indicate the minimum number of inactive pages accessed in\n *    between.\n *\n * 2. Moving one inactive page N page slots towards the tail of the\n *    list requires at least N inactive page accesses.\n *\n * Combining these:\n *\n * 1. When a page is finally evicted from memory, the number of\n *    inactive pages accessed while the page was in cache is at least\n *    the number of page slots on the inactive list.\n *\n * 2. In addition, measuring the sum of evictions and activations (E)\n *    at the time of a page's eviction, and comparing it to another\n *    reading (R) at the time the page faults back into memory tells\n *    the minimum number of accesses while the page was not cached.\n *    This is called the refault distance.\n *\n * Because the first access of the page was the fault and the second\n * access the refault, we combine the in-cache distance with the\n * out-of-cache distance to get the complete minimum access distance\n * of this page:\n *\n *      NR_inactive + (R - E)\n *\n * And knowing the minimum access distance of a page, we can easily\n * tell if the page would be able to stay in cache assuming all page\n * slots in the cache were available:\n *\n *   NR_inactive + (R - E) <= NR_inactive + NR_active\n *\n * If we have swap we should consider about NR_inactive_anon and\n * NR_active_anon, so for page cache and anonymous respectively:\n *\n *   NR_inactive_file + (R - E) <= NR_inactive_file + NR_active_file\n *   + NR_inactive_anon + NR_active_anon\n *\n *   NR_inactive_anon + (R - E) <= NR_inactive_anon + NR_active_anon\n *   + NR_inactive_file + NR_active_file\n *\n * Which can be further simplified to:\n *\n *   (R - E) <= NR_active_file + NR_inactive_anon + NR_active_anon\n *\n *   (R - E) <= NR_active_anon + NR_inactive_file + NR_active_file\n *\n * Put into words, the refault distance (out-of-cache) can be seen as\n * a deficit in inactive list space (in-cache).  If the inactive list\n * had (R - E) more page slots, the page would not have been evicted\n * in between accesses, but activated instead.  And on a full system,\n * the only thing eating into inactive list space is active pages.\n *\n *\n *\t\tRefaulting inactive pages\n *\n * All that is known about the active list is that the pages have been\n * accessed more than once in the past.  This means that at any given\n * time there is actually a good chance that pages on the active list\n * are no longer in active use.\n *\n * So when a refault distance of (R - E) is observed and there are at\n * least (R - E) pages in the userspace workingset, the refaulting page\n * is activated optimistically in the hope that (R - E) pages are actually\n * used less frequently than the refaulting page - or even not used at\n * all anymore.\n *\n * That means if inactive cache is refaulting with a suitable refault\n * distance, we assume the cache workingset is transitioning and put\n * pressure on the current workingset.\n *\n * If this is wrong and demotion kicks in, the pages which are truly\n * used more frequently will be reactivated while the less frequently\n * used once will be evicted from memory.\n *\n * But if this is right, the stale pages will be pushed out of memory\n * and the used pages get to stay in cache.\n *\n *\t\tRefaulting active pages\n *\n * If on the other hand the refaulting pages have recently been\n * deactivated, it means that the active list is no longer protecting\n * actively used cache from reclaim. This commit fills in the page_reclaim.rst stub as part of the structured
memory management documentation.

> diff --git a/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst b/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst
> index 50a30b7f8ac3..bfa53bee98c2 100644
> --- a/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/mm/page_reclaim.rst
[ ... ]
> +Working Set Detection
> +=====================
> +
> +When a page is evicted, a compact shadow entry is stored in its place in
> +the page cache or swap cache.  The shadow records the eviction timestamp
> +(in terms of the lruvec's nonresident age counter) and the cgroup and
> +node that owned the page.
> +
> +If the page is faulted back in (a "refault"), the shadow entry allows the
> +kernel to compute the *refault distance* — how many other pages were
> +activated or evicted between this page's eviction and its refault.  If the
> +refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list, the page
> +was part of the active working set and is immediately activated rather
> +than placed on the inactive list.  This reduces thrashing by protecting
> +frequently accessed pages that would otherwise be repeatedly evicted and
> +refaulted.

Is this description of the refault distance comparison accurate?

Looking at workingset_test_recent() in mm/workingset.c, the refault distance
is compared against the workingset_size (the active working set), rather
than the inactive list.

For example, for file pages, it is calculated from NR_ACTIVE_FILE and
explicitly excludes the inactive list of the same type. Could the
documentation be updated to reflect that the distance is compared against the
size of the active list?
```

> +Working Set Detection
> +=====================
> +
> +When a page is evicted, a compact shadow entry is stored in its place in

> +
> +If the page is faulted back in (a "refault"), the shadow entry allows the

> +activated or evicted between this page's eviction and its refault.  If the
> +refault distance is shorter than the size of the inactive list, the page

> +than placed on the inactive list.  This reduces thrashing by protecting

Is this description of the refault distance comparison accurate?

Looking at workingset_test_recent() in mm/workingset.c, the refault distance
is actually compared against the workingset_size (the active working set),
rather than the inactive list.

For example, for file pages, the threshold is calculated from NR_ACTIVE_FILE
and explicitly excludes the inactive list of the same type. 

Could the documentation be updated to reflect that the distance is compared
against the size of the active list instead of the inactive list?
